RNS Number : 0549B Bank of America Merrill Lynch 26 September 2025  

FORM 8.3

PUBLIC OPENING POSITION DISCLOSURE/DEALING DISCLOSURE BY

A PERSON WITH INTERESTS IN RELEVANT SECURITIES REPRESENTING 1% OR

MORE

Rule 8.3 of the Takeover Code (the "Code")

  • 1.

  • KEY INFORMATION

  • (a)

  • Full name of discloser:

Bank of America Corporation

  • (b)

  • Owner or controller of interests and short positions disclosed, if different from 1(a):

    The naming of nominee or vehicle companies is

    insufficient. For a trust, the trustee(s), settlor and

    beneficiaries must be named.

  • (c)

  • Name of offeror/offeree in relation to whose relevant securities this form relates:

    Use a separate form for each offeror/offeree

QUALCOMM INC

  • (d)

  • If an exempt fund manager connected with an offeror/offeree, state this and specify identity of offeror/offeree:

  • (e)

  • Date position held/dealing undertaken:

    For an opening position disclosure, state the latest

    practicable date prior to the disclosure

25/09/2025

  • (f)

  • In addition to the company in 1(c) above, is the

    discloser making disclosures in respect of any

    other party to the offer?

    If it is a cash offer or possible cash offer, state "N/A"

No

data.title: Warner Bros. Stock Gets Downgraded. Why Its Recent Rally Could Fade. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By George Glover

Warner Bros. Discovery stock was sliding on Friday, having caught another downgrade as uncertainty about a potential takeover by Paramount Skydance drags on.

Shares slipped 1.7% to $19.45 ahead of the opening bell. Futures tracking the S&P 500 were flat.

The move lower came after KeyBanc analyst Brandon Nispel downgraded Warner Bros. to Sector Weight from Overweight, warning that it may have become overvalued in recent weeks. The stock has surged 63% since Sept. 11, when The Wall Street Journal reported that Paramount Skydance was preparing a takeover bid. The S&P 500 has climbed just 1% over the same period.

"With the reports on a potential takeover, we feel the stock's valuation has gotten ahead of the fundamentals," Nispel wrote in a research note. "We have no way of knowing if a deal will ultimately materialize."

Nispel added that he's worried Warner Bros. could be trying to spark a bidding war, which may lead to the potential takeover collapsing. CEO David Zaslav is sounding out other bidders and is hoping for an offer of $40 a share, The New York Post reported earlier this month, citing a person with direct knowledge of the matter.

TD Cowan analyst Doug Creutz downgraded Warner Bros. earlier this month. Like Nispel, he warned that shares' stellar run since Sept. 11 may have made them overvalued.

Write to George Glover at george.glover@dowjones.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

ASTROTECH Corp SEC 10-K Report
data.description:
Astrotech Corporation, a company specializing in the development and commercialization of advanced detection and diagnostic technologies, has released its annual Form 10-K report. The report provides a comprehensive overview of the company's financial performance, business operations, strategic initiatives, and the challenges it faces in the current market environment.

Financial Highlights

Astrotech Corporation reported the following key financial metrics for the fiscal year ending June 30, 2025:

  • Total Revenue: $1.0 million, a decrease of $615 thousand or 37.0% compared to the previous year, primarily due to fewer TRACER 1000 units sold.
  • Gross Profit: $475 thousand, a decrease of $276 thousand compared to the previous year, with a slight increase in gross margin to 45.3% from 45.1%.
  • Loss from Operations: $(14.7) million, an increase of $1.5 million compared to the previous year, driven by higher research and development expenses.
  • Net Loss: $(13.9) million, an increase of $2.2 million compared to the previous year, reflecting increased operating expenses and reduced other income.
  • Total Comprehensive Loss: $(13.5) million, an increase of $2.1 million compared to the previous year, due to the factors affecting net loss.

Business Highlights

Astrotech Corporation's business performance for the fiscal year included several key developments:

  • New Product Launches: In March 2025, the company launched the enhanced TRACER 1000 Narcotic Trace Detector, designed to screen for synthetic opiates and novel psychoactive substances, enhancing narcotics trace detection capabilities.
  • New Subsidiary Formation: On February 28, 2025, Astrotech announced the creation of EN-SCAN, Inc., a new wholly owned subsidiary focused on manufacturing and selling instruments for environmental testing using proprietary technology.
  • Sales Units: As of June 30, 2025, the TRACER 1000 has been deployed in approximately 34 locations across 16 countries, including the United States, Europe, and Asia.
  • Geographical Performance: The TRACER 1000 expanded its footprint in Southeast Asia with its first sale and deployment in Vietnam, marking a significant step in the region.
  • Research and Development Contracts: In January 2025, 1st Detect Corporation was awarded a research and development contract with the U.S. Department of Homeland Security to advance the TRACER 1000 for next-generation explosives trace detection.
  • Future Outlook: Astrotech is focused on expanding the applications of its AMS Technology and growing channel partner relationships to generate revenue growth in subsequent fiscal years.
  • Operational Strategy: The company is actively pursuing TSA checkpoint testing for the TRACER 1000, which involves Developmental Test and Evaluation with the Transportation Security Laboratory to ensure readiness for certification testing.

Strategic Initiatives

Astrotech Corporation has undertaken several strategic initiatives to enhance its market position and financial stability:

  • Expansion and Market Reach: The company has focused on expanding its product offerings and market reach through the deployment of the TRACER 1000 in multiple international locations and the creation of a new subsidiary, EN-SCAN, Inc., for environmental testing instruments.
  • Research and Development: The company has secured research and development contracts with the U.S. Department of Homeland Security to advance its TRACER 1000 technology for explosives trace detection.
  • Capital Management: Historically, Astrotech has financed its operations through public offerings, raising approximately $67.6 million. As of June 30, 2025, the company held $3.1 million in cash and cash equivalents, with $15.1 million in short-term investments to support operations. The company has not paid dividends and has no plans to do so in the future.
  • Future Outlook: Astrotech anticipates increased expenses related to research and development, scaling operations, and expanding manufacturing capacity. The company plans to finance these activities through a combination of equity offerings, debt financing, and strategic partnerships. Astrotech aims to continue its growth by leveraging its proprietary technologies and expanding its market presence.

Challenges and Risks

Astrotech Corporation faces several significant risks that could impact its business operations and financial condition:

  • Financial Risks: The company has incurred significant losses since inception and anticipates continued losses for the foreseeable future. As of June 30, 2025, the accumulated deficit was approximately $251 million, with a net loss of $13.8 million for the fiscal year 2025.
  • Operational Risks: The business units are in the development stage with limited revenues, requiring substantial funding to commercialize products. The company is also heavily reliant on a limited number of customers, which poses a risk if any customer relationship is terminated.
  • Regulatory Risks: The company's products and operations are subject to extensive governmental regulation. Failure to comply with FDA requirements or obtain necessary clearances for products like the BreathTest-1000 could adversely affect business growth. Additionally, the company's AgLAB business is highly dependent on the U.S. hemp and cannabis market, which is subject to evolving regulations.
  • Intellectual Property Risks: The company may not be able to obtain patents or licenses for its technologies, and third parties have infringed on its intellectual property rights. This could lead to significant litigation expenses or prevent the sale of products.
  • Market Risks: Increased competition and the potential for technological obsolescence pose risks to the company's market position. The company must innovate and commercialize new technologies to remain competitive.
  • Supply Chain Risks: The company's manufacturing operations depend on third-party suppliers, including single-source suppliers, making it vulnerable to supply shortages and price fluctuations.
  • Geopolitical Risks: International sales expose the company to risks such as political instability, exchange rate fluctuations, and stringent foreign regulations. The ongoing Russia-Ukraine and Israel-Hamas conflicts add to these uncertainties.
  • Legal Risks: The company may face challenges in enforcing commercial agreements due to the federal illegality of cannabis, which could impact its operations and financial results.
  • Cybersecurity Risks: Increased cybersecurity threats could pose risks to the company's systems, networks, and data, potentially leading to operational disruptions and reputational damage.

Management acknowledges these challenges and is focused on strategies to mitigate them, including efforts to diversify the customer base, enhance product development, and ensure compliance with regulatory requirements. The company is also exploring additional funding options to support its growth initiatives.

SEC Filing: ASTROTECH Corp [ ASTC ] - 10-K - Sep. 26, 2025

data.title: Poste Italiane considers digital identity fee, analysts see potential 100 mln euro profit boost
data.description:
Poste Italiane is considering introducing an annual management fee for users of its digital identity service, analysts wrote in their daily reports on Friday following a briefing with the company's management.

With around 20 million active digital identity accounts, the move could generate up to 100 million euros ($116.75 million) in additional operating profit for the state-backed financial conglomerate, Intermonte and Akros analysts wrote in their report.

Poste declined to comment.

WHY IT'S IMPORTANT

The fee would mark a shift for Poste, which has so far offered these services free of charge. The change comes as other providers have begun charging for digital identity services.

CONTEXT

Sistema Pubblico di Identità Digitale (SPID) is Italy's public digital identity system, used for accessing a wide range of government and private services online. Poste is the largest SPID provider in the country.

($1 = 0.8565 euros)

data.title: Germany Is Expected to Drive Higher 2026 Eurozone Gross Bond Issuance — Market Talk
data.description: Total gross government bond issuance in the eurozone's 10 largest issuers is expected to rise in 2026 from 2025, to be led by higher German bond supply, Morgan Stanley's rates strategists say in a note. They estimate 2026 gross government bond issuance at around 1.43 trillion euros, up by around 80 billion euros or around 6% relative to 2025, they say. Supply pressures will remain more skewed toward core and semi-core countries relative to periphery, to be mostly attributable to higher borrowing needs coming from Germany, they say. Morgan Stanley strategists forecast slightly declining gross issuance in Spain, Portugal and Finland next year compared with this year. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

data.title: Rally in banking stocks still has legs
data.description:
Key points:
  • STOXX 600 up 0.6%
  • Insurers lead
  • Trump introduces more tariffs
  • Wall St futures steady

RALLY IN BANKING STOCKS STILL HAS LEGS

The banking sector (.SX7P) has been Europe's standout performer in 2025, rallying 45%, compared to an 8.5% rise for the broader STOXX 600 .

Citigroup strategists remain overweight the sector given the banks' history of share buybacks and dividends and recent earnings upgrades.

"Valuations are starting to look more stretched vs history but still look cheap vs other sectors and an all-in capital return yield of ~8% is still highly attractive," writes Citi's Andrew Coombs.

"Relative earnings momentum is particularly healthy, with +8% cons. EPS upgrades YTD, driven by non-NII & costs, whereas other European sectors are still suffering downgrades," Coombs adds.

While the sector continues to receive earnings upgrades, Coombs believes that rotation into banking stocks will persist.

Within the sector, Citi prefers stocks that offer upside to consensus EPS, attractive capital return yields and have lagged the sector re-rating year-to-date. Top picks are therefore HSBC , Intesa Sanpaolo and NatWest .

Least preferred names are Deutsche Bank , SHB and UBS .

(Samuel Indyk)

data.title: Market Talk Roundup: Latest on U.S. Politics
data.description:
Market Talks covering the impact of U.S. Politics and White House policies on companies and markets. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0813 ET - New U.S. drug tariffs shouldn't affect Belgian pharma group UCB as the company outlined plans to build a manufacturing site in the country, ING analysts write. ING says that, as far as it is aware, the 15% ceiling on U.S. levies on imports from the EU applies to branded pharmaceuticals under the recent U.S.-EU tariff deal, the analysts say. Moreover, 65% of UCB's products are manufactured by third parties worldwide, they add. UCB said in June that it would build a manufacturing plant in the U.S. that would create 300 permanent and 500 construction jobs. UCB shares rise 0.8% and are up 3.1% year-on-year. (william.gray@wsj.com)

0757 ET - It is unclear how new U.S. drug tariffs relate to a U.S.-EU trade deal capping levies, Novo Nordisk CEO Mike Doustdar says. The Danish pharma group looks forward to working with the Trump administration, but it is uncertain whether the earlier deal between the U.S. and the EU will keep tariffs at 15% rather than the 100% announced by President Trump, Doustdar writes in a statement. Construction is under way on a Novo Nordisk manufacturing site in North Carolina, he adds. Trump announced Thursday that the tariffs would hit pharmaceutical companies not building U.S. plants. Novo Nordisk shares are down 1.7%. (william.gray@wsj.com)

0405 ET - Oil prices are on track for a significant weekly gain following news that Russia plans to temporarily ban diesel exports from resellers and extend its gasoline-export ban amid Ukrainian drone strikes against refineries. In early trade, Brent crude ticks 0.1% higher to $68.66 a barrel, while WTI is up 0.2% to $65.13 a barrel. The benchmarks continue to trade in a narrow range, but are up 3% and 4.4% on the week, respectively. Geopolitical tensions are high, with European diplomats warning the Kremlin that NATO is prepared to take action against further violations of its airspace, including by shooting down Russian planes, according to a Bloomberg report. However, further gains are capped by uncertainties over the macroeconomic outlook, with President Trump saying he would slap new tariffs on pharma and large trucks, and a looming global supply surplus.(giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

0405 ET - President Trump's drug tariffs will have a limited impact on big Swiss pharma players given their U.S. presence, Vontobel analysts write. While there is broad room for interpretation of Trump's announcement, Roche and Novartis are shielded by their U.S. footprints, while Sandoz should be unaffected since it focuses on exempted generic products, the analysts say. Galderma's exposure is less clear as it has a smaller U.S. presence. Tariffs will increase U.S. drug prices and supply concerns, but they shouldn't threaten the country's leading position in drug development, Vontobel says. Galderma shares fall 3.2%, with Roche and Novartis down 0.3% and 0.2%, respectively. Sandoz shares trade broadly flat. (william.gray@wsj.com)

0337 ET - European pharmaceutical stocks fell in early trade after President Trump announced 100% tariffs on those companies that aren't building plants in the U.S. Swiss skincare company Galderma was the biggest faller, losing 3.55%, while Novo Nordisk shares are down 1.3%. AstraZeneca, Roche and Bayer shares were all down 0.2%. Tariffs are expected to start from Oct. 1.(ian.walker@wsj.com)

0318 ET - President Trump's announcement of 25% tariffs on imported heavy trucks is light on detail, but Daimler Truck could suffer the most given current production in the U.S. and overall exposure to the U.S. at the group level, Bernstein analysts write. U.S. manufacturer Paccar could be the greatest beneficiary, it adds. Bernstein notes the tariffs apply from Oct. 1, so they won't impact the third quarter, where orders and volumes are weak for all manufacturers. It adds that further inflationary increases to truck prices will negatively impact all, especially with new truck prices currently considerably elevated after many years of price increases. It rates Volvo and Daimler Truck at underperform and Traton at market-perform. Volvo shares rise 2.1%, Daimler Truck shares fall 4.4% and Traton shares drop 2.1%. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0301 ET - The dollar turns lower after President Trump announced a new round of tariffs, including a 100% tariff on drugs by companies that aren't building plants in the U.S. as well as levies on large trucks and home goods, beginning Oct. 1. The dollar had jumped to a three-week high against a basket of currencies on Thursday after a string of strong U.S. economic data, including a sizeable upgrade to U.S. second-quarter GDP. This damped prospects of interest-rate cuts. However, Commerzbank analyst Michael Pfister says the U.S. economy's dependence on private consumption poses a risk if sentiment declines. Trump's "erratic trade policy is doing much to dampen consumer sentiment," he says. The DXY dollar index falls 0.2% to 98.409 after hitting a high of 98.605 on Thursday. (jessica.fleetham@wsj.com)

0300 ET - The market likely doesn't believe the U.S.'s pharmaceutical tariffs will have real, significant bite in the end, says Citi Wealth's Ken Peng in a media roundtable. The head of Asia investment strategy notes President Trump offered an "escape" for pharma manufacturers, as they will be exempted from the tariffs as long as they start to build a plant in the U.S. While Asian pharma names fell after the news, Peng says: "If people really believed that large swaths of branded pharma would be tariffed at 100%, [the shares' movement] wouldn't be a 3%-4% correction... I think the market is calling it bluff." The new tariffs are also unlikely to hurt U.S.-China trade negotiations much, which he views as more important. (megan.cheah@wsj.com)

0239 ET - The impact of U.S. medical-device tariffs on European med-tech companies hinges on whether products for disabled people will be exempted, Bernstein analysts write. Products for disabled people are exempted from tariffs under the Nairobi Protocol and the effect of tariffs on the sector will depend on whether levies can override it, according to Bernstein. If applicable, chronic-care specialists including Coloplast and ConvaTec would be more exposed to potential levies than hearing-aid makers including Sonova, Demant, GN Store Nord and Amplifon, the analysts say. Another critical factor is manufacturing location. A recent U.S.-EU trade agreement caps tariffs on medicines at 15%, but it is unclear if similar agreements would shield medical technologies, Bernstein writes. Convatec fell 5.6%, Demant 4.8%, and GN 6.6% on Thursday. (william.gray@wsj.com)

0121 ET - The impact of U.S. trade policy on Japan's corporate profits, investment and wage growth could be limited if Japanese companies continue serving U.S. customers through their local subsidiaries, Capital Economics says. While BOJ data suggest higher U.S. tariffs have weighed on Japanese exports, the September manufacturing PMI subindex for new export orders indicates export growth is unlikely to weaken much further, notes Asia-Pacific head Marcel Thieliant. He adds that Japanese companies are already mitigating tariffs by ramping up production in their U.S. subsidiaries, with Japan's U.S.-bound FDI on track to hit a record high this year. However, Thieliant suggests that this strength in FDI likely reflects U.S. economic outperformance rather than trade-policy changes. (jason.chau@wsj.com)

0030 ET - After a period of calm on the tariff front, U.S. President Trump announces another round of sectoral tariffs to take effect Oct. 1. Gauging the impact of the pharmaceutical tariffs is difficult, as many big drug companies already have U.S. facilities and have announced expansions, says Shane Oliver, chief economist at AMP. But the further tariffs, with more still to come on products like semiconductors, highlight that the tariff threat has not gone away, he adds. The latest announcements take the average U.S. tariff rate before any substitution to around 21%, well down from post-Liberation Day highs but well up from about 2.5% at the start of the year, Oliver says. (james.glynn@wsj.com; @JamesGlynnWSJ)

2003 ET - A quick finalization of South Korea's $350 billion U.S. investment fund deal in exchange for lowering U.S. tariffs is less likely, says Citigroup analyst Jin-Wook Kim in a note. Seoul and Washington have struggled to work out details of the deal, verbally agreed on in July but yet to be formally signed. Both sides could either reach a partial compromise after a prolonged deadlock or see the U.S. impose an earlier proposed 25% or even higher tariff on Korean goods if they fail to reach a final agreement during the Oct. 31-Nov. 1 APEC Korea Summit, which Trump is reportedly attending, Kim says. (kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com)

data.title: Global Equities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Equities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0813 ET - New U.S. drug tariffs shouldn't affect Belgian pharma group UCB as the company outlined plans to build a manufacturing site in the country, ING analysts write. ING says that, as far as it is aware, the 15% ceiling on U.S. levies on imports from the EU applies to branded pharmaceuticals under the recent U.S.-EU tariff deal, the analysts say. Moreover, 65% of UCB's products are manufactured by third parties worldwide, they add. UCB said in June that it would build a manufacturing plant in the U.S. that would create 300 permanent and 500 construction jobs. UCB shares rise 0.8% and are up 3.1% year-on-year. (william.gray@wsj.com)

0809 ET - Tesco is expected to raise its dividend when it reports half-year earnings Thursday, AJ Bell analysts write. Consensus estimates suggest the U.K.'s number-one grocer by market share will increase its full-year payout to 14.2 pence a share, from 13.7 pence, they say, adding that it therefore seems logical to expect an increase in the interim distribution. Analysts say that sales-growth momentum will be on the radar after Tesco reported a 4.7% increase in the U.K. for the first quarter. Still, AJ Bell notes analysts caution over the company's profit forecast given the current price war among grocers and says higher costs from wages and national insurance payments are likely to hurt its full-year operating margin. Shares are up 1.5% at 441 pence and 20% higher over the year to date. (aimee.look@wsj.com)

0757 ET - It is unclear how new U.S. drug tariffs relate to a U.S.-EU trade deal capping levies, Novo Nordisk CEO Mike Doustdar says. The Danish pharma group looks forward to working with the Trump administration, but it is uncertain whether the earlier deal between the U.S. and the EU will keep tariffs at 15% rather than the 100% announced by President Trump, Doustdar writes in a statement. Construction is under way on a Novo Nordisk manufacturing site in North Carolina, he adds. Trump announced Thursday that the tariffs would hit pharmaceutical companies not building U.S. plants. Novo Nordisk shares are down 1.7%. (william.gray@wsj.com)

0748 ET - The recent surge in Warner Bros. Discovery doesn't account for the fact that a buyout bid from Paramount Skydance may never materialize, say KeyBanc analysts Brandon Nispel and Matt Sbriglio, who downgrade the stock to sector weight. Warner Bros. Discovery shares have risen to nearly $20 from $12.54 on reports of a potential takeover offer from Paramount Skydance, but if a deal doesn't happen, shares would likely return to their original levels, the analysts speculate. "The stock's valuation has gotten ahead of the fundamentals," the analysts say. "We have no way to know if a deal will ultimately materialize." Warner Bros. Discovery is off 1.5% to $19.47 premarket. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

0724 ET - Expectations for Tesco are high ahead of its half-year earnings Thursday and any forecast upgrades will be warmly received, Interactive Investor's Richard Hunter writes. Alongside first-quarter figures in June the number-one U.K. grocer by market share maintained its guidance for adjusted operating profit of between 2.7 billion and 3 billion pounds for the year, he says. Tesco's market share is relatively protected despite a price war heating up among grocery chains in the U.K. Asda, which is the third largest grocer by market share, said in spring that it would lower prices to regain lost market share. "Try as they may, other supermarkets have tended to take market share from each other rather than from Tesco," he adds. Shares are up 1.5% at 441 pence and 20% higher over the year-to-date.(aimee.look@wsj.com)

0707 ET - A German business group representing chemical and pharmaceutical companies is urging the EU to push for an agreement with President Trump on tariffs for medicines. VCI says it is very concerned about Trump's plan to impose an 100% tariff on pharmaceutical imports as of October. The move comes after the U.S. and the EU struck a deal this summer for a 15% U.S. tariff on medicines coming from the bloc. VCI CEO Wolfgang Grosse Entrup says the tariff announcement is a low point for EU-U.S. trade relations. The U.S. is an important market for the German pharmaceutical industry, with exports amounting to 27.9 billion euros in 2024, he adds. The Stoxx Europe 600 Health Care index is up 0.1%. (aimee.look@wsj.com)

0646 ET - Shares in European steel companies rise on Friday following reports that the EU Commission will impose 25% to 50% tariffs on Chinese steel and related products. The tariffs come as President Trump closes off the U.S. market, leading China to divert large quantities of goods to Europe and pushing down prices, German newspaper Handelsblatt first reported Thursday. Shares in German steel producers Thyssenkrupp and Salzgitter rise 1.8% and 4.1%, respectively, while Austrian peer Voestalpine rises 2.5%. Arcelormittal and Helsinki-headquartered Outokumpu both rise 1.7%. In Spain, Acerinox rises 1.7%. "This goes to show the U.S. might not be the only winner from tariffs - others are deploying the same strategy with foreign trading partners to protect domestic producers," AJ Bell's Russ Mould says in a market comment. (nina.kienle@wsj.com)

0646 ET - Airbus is losing ground as a defense stock due to a governance dispute inside the Future Combat Aircraft System (FCAS), an European project to build a sixth-generation fighter jet, Mwb Research's Jens-Peter Rieck says. The program is paralyzed by governance disputes between France's Dassault Aviation, which insists on a dominant role and threatens to build a jet alone, and its German and Spanish partners Airbus and Indra Sistemas, Rieck says. Airbus's role in the program is increasingly untenable, the analyst adds. "Airbus Defense and Space remains stuck in a support role, lacking proprietary fighter IP...Instead of elevating Airbus as Europe's defense champion, FCAS exposes its structural weakness." Mwb Research cuts its rating for Airbus's stock to sell from hold, and keeps its target price at 170 euros. Shares are down 0.1% at 193.60 euros. (cristina.gallardo@wsj.com)

0627 ET - Teleperformance looks cheap, but also comes with significant risk, RBC Capital Markets analysts Karl Green and Andrew Brooke say in a research note. Teleperformance's competitor Concentrix's earnings miss spooked the market Thursday, showing investor's zero tolerance for slip-ups in the customer-experience sector, the analysts say. Concentrix fell as much as 21% after the third-quarter update. While the industry is the most susceptible to AI disruption, outsourcing demand, continuing consolidation and tech-driven efficiency still support the long-term case, the analysts note. Teleperformance shares fall 4.4% to 61.18 euros and are down 26.4% year-to-date. (maitane.sardon@wsj.com)

0547 ET - Taiwan's decision to restrict chip exports to South Africa, which has since been paused, could alarm other countries, economists at Capital Economics say in a note. Taiwan suspended the restriction on Thursday, two days after the initial order, as the two sides agreed to discuss a dispute regarding Taiwan's representative office in the African country. "The threat raises questions about whether such curbs should now be considered part of Taiwan's armory in managing diplomatic ties," they say. Taiwan's dominant role in the semiconductor supply chain gives it some negotiating power, they say. Although Taiwan has paused the export ban, the perception that Taiwan might take advantage of its advanced chips for diplomatic goals could urge countries to reduce their dependence on Taiwanese chips in the future, the economists add. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

0544 ET - Crowne Plaza and Holiday Inn owner InterContinental Hotels Group is a quality business benefiting from superior earnings visibility, JPMorgan analysts write. IHG has a defensive business model compared with other European hotels and has made progress in net growth in available rooms, they add. The analysts raised their rating on the stock to overweight from underweight and target price to 10.40 pounds from 8.50 pounds. IHG's stock has underperformed this year at 15% lower than the MSCI Leisure index, due to its U.S. exposure, currency effects and weaker revenue per available room, they say. However, the analysts expect this to turn around as revenue per available room expectations have now been revised. Shares are up 2.8% at 9.03 pounds, leading the FTSE 100 index risers. (aimee.look@wsj.com)

0516 ET - The U.S. dollar could struggle to rise much further as investors turn cautious ahead of next week's key jobs data, BlueBay Asset Management CIO Mark Dowding says in a note. The dollar rose to a three-week high against a basket of currencies on Thursday after a string of strong U.S. data. It had been on the verge of breaking out of its recent trading range but has since stalled. "This could infer further sideways price action in the short term," he says. Weak jobs data next week could give a renewed impetus towards a weaker dollar, which is in line with BlueBay's medium-term view. The DXY dollar index falls 0.2% to 98.373, having hit a high of 98.605 on Thursday. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

data.title: India to gross borrow 6.77 trillion rupees in October-March
data.description:
The Indian government will raise 6.77 trillion rupees ($76.32 billion) through market borrowings during the October to March period, marginally lower than its previously announced schedule in its effort to tame rising bond yields and ensure market stability.

India had planned to raise 14.82 trillion rupees in the current financial year through March, and sold bonds worth 7.95 trillion rupees in April-September.

The government has reduce the share of ultra-long bonds to 29.5% in second half, down from 35% in first half, while it has also included 100 billion rupees of green bond sales.

($1 = 88.7110 Indian rupees)

data.title: Stocks pressured by Fed rate cut doubts, new Trump tariffs
data.description:
Key points:
  • Trump announces new tariffs from October 1
  • Pharmaceutical stocks fall in Asia, most indexes down
  • Fed easing expectations recede on US economic resilience
  • Dollar headed for weekly gain

By Naomi Rovnick and Rae Wee

Anxiety rippled across previously buoyant global markets on Friday, as traders cautiously awaited U.S. inflation data, while fresh White House tariffs on pharmaceuticals weighed heavily on Asian bourses and public financing concerns weighed on sterling.

MSCI's index of global stocks EURONEXT:IACWI headed for its first weekly loss in four after U.S. President Donald Trump unveiled 100% duties on imported branded drugs as well as new tariffs on goods from trucks to kitchen cabinets.

A broad gauge of Asian shares outside Japan (.MIAPJ0000PUS) closed 1.4% lower, led by falls in South Korea and Hong Kong as Japanese stocks closed flat and mainland Chinese shares (..CSI000300) lost almost 1%.

Europe's regional STOXX 600 share index traded 0.3% higher as major European drugmakers like Roche and Novo Nordisk recovered from initial losses and traders sought shelter from U.S. interest rate uncertainty in euro zone assets.

Ahead of the latest reading of the U.S. personal consumption expenditures (PCE) index later in the day, which functions as a key component of the Federal Reserve's inflation outlook, euro zone bonds also outperformed Treasuries.

Wall Street's benchmark S&P 500 share index has notched up 25 record closing highs over the past three months as rate cut fervour combined with a wave of fresh enthusiasm for AI stocks and investors backed out of international markets.

Ahead of U.S. bourses opening for cash trading on Friday however, futures markets indicated the S&P 500 would trade sideways and the tech-heavy Nasdaq 100 would inch 0.1% lower , , leaving both indexes flat for the week.

FED RATE CUT BETS RECEDE

"You're not getting that scenario where hard economic data is flowing through to confirm (U.S.) rate cuts," Premier Miton fund manager Daniel Hughes said.

On Thursday, U.S. second quarter economic growth data was revised sharply higher to a 3.8% annualised rate, from 3.3% previously, and a slight drop in weekly jobless claims signaled the labour market was strengthening.

Germany's 10-year Bund yield, which falls as the price of the benchmark euro zone debt security rises, was down 2 basis points (bps) at 2.756% as the equivalent U.S. Treasury yield was stuck at 4.1736%, close to its highest so far this month.

After the U.S. central bank lowered borrowing costs by 25 bps last week, new Fed appointee and Trump ally Stephen Miran pressed for ongoing cuts to prevent labour market collapse but multiple other Fed policymakers expressed caution on inflation.

Traders are pricing in about 39 basis points worth of rate cuts by December, compared to more than 40 bps earlier this week. (0#USDIRPR).

STERLING REMAINS UNDER PRESSURE

Stress in UK markets persisted on Friday as sterling (GPB=D3) headed for its biggest weekly decline in two months as it traded at $1.3348, the lowest level since early August.

UK gilts broke out of their usual negative feedback loop with sterling, with the yield on the benchmark 10-year UK debt security (UK10YT=RR) dropping 4 bps to 4.729%.

Benchmark UK borrowing costs hit their highest since early July in the prior session, pressuring the UK's strained public finances because rising debt yields compromise finance minister Rachel Reeves' ability to stick to self-imposed fiscal rules.

With the next UK Budget in November analysts are already speculating about how Reeves may seek out fresh sources of revenue, with Deutsche Bank analysts on Friday suggesting she may end a long-term freeze on petrol duties.

This, they said, could be inflationary and would require "tighter monetary policy that reduces growth."

Elsewhere in markets, the dollar was steady against a basket of major currencies including the euro and the yen but was on track for a 0.8% weekly gain and its best performance on this measure since late July, as Fed rate cut hopes receded.

The yen languished near the 150-per-dollar level and was headed for a weekly fall of more than 1%, while the euro last bought $1.1673.

In commodities, Brent crude oil futures headed for a more than 4% gain on the week, buoyed by robust U.S. growth data.

Safe haven gold which hit an all-time peak of $3,790 an ounce earlier this week, had drifted back to $3,750 by the end of the European morning on Friday.

data.title: Green Rain Energy Holdings (OTCID:$GREH) Announces New EV Sites With Driftwood Hospitality

PASADENA, CA / ACCESS Newswire / September 26, 2025 / Green Rain Energy Holdings (OTCID:$GREH) announces deployment of next-gen EV charging stations at both the Marriott Albany and the Saratoga Springs Hilton. Both hotels are operated by Driftwood Hospitality Management. This move underscores Green Rain Energy's commitment to accelerating the adoption of sustainable energy, reducing carbon footprints, and providing travelers with reliable, high-speed charging solutions nationwide.

Driftwood Hospitality, one of the largest hotel management groups in America, has pinpointed multiple locations for immediate installation. This program requires zero out-of-pocket costs for hotel owners, as Green Rain Energy will oversee installation, operations, and maintenance of the charging infrastructure. Green Rain has created a seamless solution for both property owners and guests.

With Green Rain Energy's channel partner Chronicle Electric, the Company has launched long-term projects aimed at modernizing existing charging networks. These advanced level 3 chargers and cutting-edge software will provide guests with a smooth, fast, and highly reliable charging experience.

"These two new sites in Saratoga Springs and Albany are just the latest commitment we are making to the state of New York," said Alfredo Papadakis, CEO of Green Rain Energy. "We are committed to creating more opportunities for our investors and channel partners with every announcement."

data.title: GTCR to Buy Dentalcorp in Deal Valuing Dental Network at About $1.58 Billion
data.description:
By Colin Kellaher

Dentalcorp Holdings has agreed to be acquired by buyout shop GTCR in a deal that values the network of dental practices at about 2.2 billion Canadian dollars ($1.58 billion).

Dentalcorp on Friday said its shareholders will receive C$11 a share in cash, a nearly 33% premium to Thursday's closing price of C$8.28 for the Toronto company.

Dentalcorp said Graham Rosenberg, its founder, chairman and chief executive, and Nate Tchaplia, its president and finance chief, have agreed to roll part of their holdings into the acquired company, as has private-equity firm L Catterton. The latter invested in dentalcorp in 2018 and owns a roughly 37% stake, according to data from FactSet.

The deal is slated to close in the first quarter of 2026.

Chicago-based GTCR has invested more than $30 billion in over 290 companies since its inception and currently manages about $50 billion in equity capital.

Write to Colin Kellaher at colin.kellaher@wsj.com

data.title: Stearman Resources Announces Private Placement
data.description:
(TheNewswire)

VANCOUVER, BRITISH COLUMBIA – TheNewswire - September 26, 2025 –
Stearman Resources Inc. (CSE:STMN) (“Stearman” or the
“Company”) announces a
non-brokered private placement of up to 10,000,000 common shares (the
"Shares"), at a price of $0.15 per Share for gross proceeds of
up to $1,500,000 (the "Offering").


The Company will also be offering up to 16,000,000
flow-through common shares (the ”Flow-through Shares”), at a price of $0.25 per Flow-through Share for gross
proceeds of up to $4,000,000 (the “Flow-through Offering”).


In connection with the Offering and the Flow-through Offering, the
Company will pay to qualified finders, a 6% cash finders fee and a 6%
finder’s fee payable in non-transferable share purchase warrants.
Each warrant entitling the holder thereof to purchase one common share
in the capital of the Company at a price of $0.50 per share for a
period of twenty-four (24) months from closing of the Offering and
Flow-through Offering.


The gross proceeds from the sale of the Flow-through Shares will be
used by the Company to incur eligible “Canadian exploration
expenses” that will qualify as “flow-through mining
expenditures” as such terms are defined in the Income Tax Act (Canada) (the
“Qualifying Expenditures“)
related to the Company’s projects in Canada.


All securities issued pursuant to the Offering will be
subject to a hold period of four months and one day as required under
applicable securities legislation.


The securities issued pursuant to
the Offering have not been, nor will they be, registered under the
United States Securities Act of 1933, as amended, and may not be
offered or sold within the United States or to, or for the account or
benefit of, U.S. persons in the absence of U.S. registration or an
applicable exemption from the U.S. registration requirements. This
news release shall not constitute an offer to sell or the solicitation
of an offer to buy nor shall there be any sale of the securities in
the United States or in any other jurisdiction in which such offer,
solicitation or sale would be unlawful.


About
Stearman


The Company is a mineral
exploration company focused on the acquisition, exploration and
development of mineral properties in Canada and the USA. The Company
currently owns a 100% interest in the NeoCore Uranium Property
consisting of 6 mineral claims covering 13,012 hectares, located in
the Athabasca Basin in northern Saskatchewan.  The Company further
holds an option for up to a 70% interest in the Murphy Lake Uranium
Property, consisting of 609 hectares also located in the Athabasca
Basin in northern Saskatchewan, and now owns a 100% interest in the
Zoo Bay Uranium Property consisting of 15
mineral claims covering 19,850 hectares, also located in the Athabasca
Basin.

data.title: FAA to Ease Restrictions on Boeing Aircraft Deliveries — WSJ
data.description:
By Andrew Tangel and Drew FitzGerald

Federal regulators are scaling back obstacles for Boeing to deliver some of its newly produced aircraft to customers, a hopeful sign for the plane maker's recovery from a string of crises.

Boeing is slated to regain authority from the Federal Aviation Administration to perform final safety checks on its 737 MAX jets, people familiar with the matter said. The approval could be announced as soon as Friday, one person said.

Employees of the aerospace giant would regain the authority to perform safety signoffs in phases, the people said. FAA inspectors will remain involved in issuing so-called airworthiness certificates, which are required for each aircraft to be able to fly with passengers.

The FAA often delegates routine inspections to aerospace manufacturers to focus scarce personnel on more critical tasks. But regulators tightened oversight of Boeing in 2019 after two deadly 737 MAX crashes.

The FAA began requiring each new jet rolling out of the 737 MAX factory in Renton, Wash., to be checked only by agency inspectors. The federal agency had become concerned about pressure on Boeing employees to produce and deliver aircraft. It said it needed to more closely monitor the manufacturing process to ensure the safety of the flying public.

Later, after a series of quality mishaps, the FAA revoked the same authority for the 787 Dreamliner, which is made in South Carolina. Boeing is also expected to regain the ability on the Dreamliner, some of the people said.

The restriction has meant Boeing can't be as flexible with delivering planes to customers, having to work on government employees' workday schedules to get safety approvals. Returning some of the authority to Boeing could free up FAA inspectors to more closely monitor the production of planes, rather than paperwork at the end of the process.

Boeing's easing regulatory burden is a sign that government officials are increasingly satisfied with the company's effort to improve its manufacturing process.

The FAA is also expected to allow Boeing to boost its 737 MAX production rate to 42 planes a month, up from a cap of 38, people familiar with the matter said. Regulators, blaming Boeing's emphasis on production over quality, imposed the production limit after a fuselage panel blew off an Alaska Airlines jet midair in early 2024.

It couldn't be learned when the FAA would make a final decision. Lifting the cap would allow Boeing to more quickly produce and deliver aircraft.

If it is allowed to raise monthly 737 MAX production to 42 jets, the company has said, it would hold production at that level for several months to watch for any issues. After that time, the company would pick up the monthly pace by another five jets.

"Obviously, there's strong demand for our portfolio, and the more we can accelerate the production rate, the better," Chief Executive Kelly Ortberg said during an investor conference earlier this month. "However...we are not going to push. If we're not ready, we'll wait a month."

Boeing's ability to conduct safety checks on its planes has been politically sensitive.

Frontline FAA employees and managers, as well as some senior agency officials, had wanted to return the authority to Boeing before the Alaska Airlines door-plug blowout in 2024.

Before the incident, the quality of Boeing's airplanes had been improving, according to agency officials at that time. Still, returning the authority came with complicated optics for the FAA, whose oversight has been criticized as weak after the plane maker's various crises.

The Alaska Airlines accident ensured Boeing would remain under tighter regulatory scrutiny for longer. Employees at Boeing's Renton factory failed to bolt a fuselage panel in place before it left the site. The FAA imposed a production limit, established key performance metrics, added inspectors to Boeing's factory and re-established a permanent presence there.

The FAA in early September proposed Boeing pay $3.1 million in fines for alleged safety violations partly in connection with the Alaska blowout. Among the problems flagged by the FAA: Boeing presented two aircraft with problems to the FAA for final approval and failed to follow its quality system.
